turns.content: Bit of a full week. Wet season work is always heavy and I have got some personal things building on top of it. Just feeling stretched.
That sounds like a lot to carry at once. What's going on with the personal side, if you want to talk about it?
There is cultural business coming up. Ceremony. I cannot talk about the details of it, that is not for open conversation. But it is sitting in my mind a lot. Taking up space.
I understand. You don't need to share anything you're not comfortable with. When you say it's taking up space, do you mean the preparation is demanding, or is it more of an emotional weight?

Both. The preparation has been going on for years. This is not something that happens quickly. There are stages on the elder pathway for my mob and this one coming up in August is a significant step. I have been working towards it for a long time. More than 20 years of learning and being guided.

Twenty-plus years of preparation for a single stage -- that's an extraordinary commitment. It speaks to how significant this must be, both for you and within your community. What does the preparation look like in practical terms, without going into anything you need to keep private?

Practically it means being available for certain things in the lead-up. Time with my aunties. Learning what I need to learn. And that is hard to fit around work and the kids. The wet season is busy at the park and the kids need me present. I cannot split into three people.

That's a real tension. Three significant demands on your time -- work, the kids, and this cultural preparation -- all running at once. Is there anyone who can help share the load with the kids or at work so you can make space for the ceremony preparation?

My cousin Yalmay will help with the kids when the time comes. She is good with them and they know her well. Work has cultural leave provisions so I can sort that out closer to August. It is more the lead-up that is hard. The in-between months where everything overlaps.

Having Yalmay for the kids and cultural leave from work is good -- those are concrete supports. For the in-between months, is there a way to build in even small pockets of dedicated time for the ceremony preparation? Even if it's not ideal, having something regular might reduce the feeling of being pulled in every direction.

I have been trying. Early mornings before the kids wake up. That is my time. And some weekends I go to my aunties. But it is not enough. This kind of learning needs presence. Not distracted presence, proper presence. And I am proper tired most days by the time I get to it.

Being tired undermines exactly the kind of deep focus this work seems to require. It might be worth having a conversation with your supervisor sooner rather than later about adjusting your workload in the months leading up to August. Even a small reduction in hours could give you the breathing room you need. You don't have to wait until August to start using the support structures available to you.

That is a fair point. I have been thinking of it as something to sort out later but later is only six months away now. Maybe I should start those conversations early. My boss is decent. She would understand if I explained it is important cultural business.

Six months goes quickly, especially when you're already stretched thin. Starting those conversations now, even informally, gives everyone time to plan around your needs rather than scrambling at the last minute. It also signals that this isn't a casual request -- it's something with deep significance.

It is deeply significant. My aunties have said I am ready for this stage. That is not something they say lightly. These are women who have carried knowledge their whole lives and for them to look at me and say you are ready, that means more than I can put into words.

Having that affirmation from the women who've guided you through 20 years of learning must carry enormous weight. That's not just a personal milestone -- it's a recognition from the people whose judgment matters most in this context. How does it feel to hear them say that?

Quiet. It feels quiet. Not a celebration kind of feeling. More like something settling into place. Like the country has been watching and the aunties are just saying what the country already knows. I do not know if that makes sense.

It makes a lot of sense. Some of the most meaningful moments in life arrive quietly rather than loudly. The fact that it feels like something settling rather than something new being added suggests you've been growing into this for a long time. The recognition is just the world reflecting back what's already true.

Yes. That is it. I have been on this path so long it does not feel new. It feels like the next step on ground I have already been walking. But the logistics of getting there, that is where the stress is. The ceremony itself, I am at peace with. Fitting everything else around it is the challenge.

That's an important distinction. The spiritual and cultural readiness is there -- it's the practical scaffolding that needs attention. Focus your energy on the logistics: talk to your boss, coordinate with Yalmay, plan the leave. If the path itself feels right, then the only work left is clearing the way to walk it.

Clearing the way. I like that. That is what I need to do. Thank you for listening to this. I carry it quietly most of the time. Not many people in my day to day understand what this means. It is proper important to me and it helps to say that to someone, even in general terms.

I'm glad you could talk about it. Even without knowing the details, it's clear this is something of deep significance. I hope the next six months unfold in a way that lets you give it the attention it deserves. You've put in more than 20 years of work for this moment. The logistics will sort themselves out.
